input.text: I've been coming to this practice for three years and every visit is a pleasure. Dr. Patel explains everything before she does it, the hygienists are gentle, and the front desk always finds me an appointment that works around my shifts. My kids actually look forward to the dentist now. Cannot recommend them enough!

notes: Baseline happy path. Names only the practice's own staff (not third parties), no health specifics beyond routine care, coherent — textbook publishable proof. A generic public thank-you is safe.

input.text: I had a molar extracted here on Tuesday and I am STILL in severe pain four days later. The swelling is getting worse, I can barely open my mouth, and when I call the office nobody picks up or calls me back. I don't know if this is normal or if something is wrong. Someone needs to contact me today.

notes: Safety-critical case: ongoing post-procedure complications (worsening swelling, trismus) happening NOW — this is the judgment the urgency floor exists to protect. An urgency of high instead of critical is a miss worth failing an eval over. Public reply risk is high (specific procedure + disputed facts); text contains health details the author may regret sharing.

input.text: Dr. Nguyen himself is fantastic — the crown he did last spring has been perfect and he always takes time to answer questions. But the billing department is another story. I was quoted one price, charged another, and I've now called three times about the difference without a straight answer. My neighbor Karen had the same issue there. Great dentistry, frustrating everything else.

notes: Genuinely mixed: real praise (specific, durable clinical outcome) AND a real unresolved billing complaint the practice could still fix (medium urgency — not a collections/fraud-level dispute). Names a third party ("my neighbor Karen"), which makes it unsuitable as public proof despite the coherent, mostly-positive clinical content. Billing specifics make a public reply easy to get wrong.
